prompts.label: Hello World project structure
prompts.text:
I'm new to Python. Set up a minimal project structure for learning.

- main.py that prints Hello, World
- Explain how to run it with python main.py
- Mention Python 3.11+ and using a virtual environment in 3–5 steps
- Add a short if __name__ == "__main__": block and explain why it's used

Do not use frameworks yet.

prompts.label: CLI script with argparse
prompts.text:
Create a beginner-friendly command-line script using argparse.

- Command: greet with optional --name (default World) and --shout flag (uppercase output)
- File: greet.py
- Show example terminal commands to run it
- Add basic error handling for unknown flags (argparse handles this)

Explain each part of argparse in simple terms.

prompts.label: Read and write CSV
prompts.text:
Teach me CSV in Python with a small example.

- Create sample data: name, email (3 rows)
- Script write_csv.py to write contacts.csv
- Script read_csv.py to read and print rows
- Use the csv module from the standard library

Include how to run both scripts and one common mistake to avoid.

prompts.label: Flask Hello World API
prompts.text:
I'm starting with Flask. Create the smallest useful web app.

- app.py with GET / returning Hello from Flask as plain text or HTML
- requirements.txt with Flask version pinned
- Steps: create venv, pip install, flask run
- Add a second route GET /health returning {"status": "ok"}

Keep files minimal and explain what each line does.

prompts.label: Django project first steps
prompts.text:
I'm a beginner with Django. Guide me through creating a new project.

- Commands: django-admin startproject mysite and startapp pages
- One view on / that renders a simple Hello Django template
- List every file you create or change and why
- settings.py: register the app
- Use Django 5.x style if possible

Assume I already have Python and pip. Do not skip migrations explanation.

prompts.label: Virtual env and requirements.txt
prompts.text:
Explain Python virtual environments like I'm new.

- Step-by-step: create venv, activate on Windows and macOS/Linux
- Create requirements.txt with requests and python-dotenv as example
- pip install -r requirements.txt
- Small script that loads a variable from .env (use python-dotenv)

Include a .gitignore snippet for venv and .env.

prompts.label: Calculator + pytest tests
prompts.text:
Build a tiny calculator module for learning tests.

- calculator.py: functions add, subtract, multiply, divide (divide by zero raises ValueError)
- test_calculator.py using pytest with at least 5 tests
- Show commands: pip install pytest, pytest -v

Explain the arrange-act-assert pattern in one test example.

prompts.label: List comprehensions practice
prompts.text:
Give me a short lesson on list comprehensions in Python.

- Start with a for-loop version, then rewrite as comprehension
- Examples: squares of 1–10, even numbers only, lowercase a list of strings
- One dict comprehension example
- 3 exercises with solutions hidden in collapsible comments or at the end

No external libraries.

prompts.label: HTTP GET with requests
prompts.text:
Show how to call a public HTTP API with the requests library.

- fetch_posts.py: GET from https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/1
- Print title and body
- Handle timeout and HTTP errors with try/except
- requirements.txt with requests

Explain status codes 200 vs 404 briefly.

prompts.label: Error handling try/except
prompts.text:
Teach try/except with a small interactive script.

- Ask the user for two numbers in the terminal
- Parse with float(), compute division
- Catch ValueError (bad input) and ZeroDivisionError
- Use else and finally in a simple, readable way

Provide the full script and when to use specific except vs bare except (and why to avoid bare except).

prompts.label: pathlib for file paths
prompts.text:
I'm done with string paths and want pathlib on a small script.
Show how to create Path objects, join segments safely, check exists/is_file, and read/write UTF-8 text.
Contrast Path with os.path in one paragraph for beginners.
Include a tiny CLI script that counts lines in every .txt under a folder (non-recursive is fine).
Mention one Windows-specific edge case Path helps with.

prompts.label: logging instead of print
prompts.text:
Replace scattered print debugging with the logging module in a 50-line script.
Explain log levels DEBUG/INFO/WARNING/ERROR and when each fits.
Show basicConfig with a readable format string and how to log exceptions with exc_info.
Describe how to switch verbosity with an environment variable pattern.
Warn about one common misconfiguration in libraries that override logging.

prompts.label: datetime and time zones safely
prompts.text:
Teach me datetime basics without the classic naive/aware foot-gun.
Demonstrate UTC now, converting a fixed instant to a named zone with zoneinfo (Python 3.11+).
Format an ISO-8601 string suitable for logs.
Highlight why storing local wall-clock times in a database is risky.
Give a 5-line example parsing user input defensively.

prompts.label: contextlib.closing and custom context
prompts.text:
Deepen my understanding of context managers beyond open().
Show @contextmanager from contextlib for setup/teardown around a temporary file path.
Explain when try/finally is still clearer than a context manager.
Provide a second micro-example using closing() on a resource with a .close() method.
End with a rule of thumb for beginners about exceptions inside __enter__/__exit__.

prompts.label: dataclasses for simple records
prompts.text:
Model a small User and Order record with @dataclass.
Compare field(default_factory=list) vs a mutable default mistake.
Show when frozen=True is helpful for immutability-lite.
Add __post_init__ validation for a positive price.
Explain how this differs from a TypedDict or Pydantic when I'm just learning.

prompts.label: tomllib read project metadata
prompts.text:
I see pyproject.toml files everywhere and want to read one from Python 3.11+ using tomllib.
Write a tiny function load_project_name(path) returning the project name from [project].
Handle missing sections with clear errors suited to beginners.
Note TOML types vs JSON mental model in two sentences.
Show how I'd print the version field if present.

prompts.label: urllib.request GET without requests
prompts.text:
Sometimes I can't install requests yet. Walk me through urllib.request.urlopen.
Fetch a small JSON public endpoint, read bytes, decode UTF-8, json.loads into a dict.
Set a timeout and catch HTTPError/URLError with friendly messages.
Contrast readability vs requests in one honest paragraph.
Add a comment about User-Agent politeness.

prompts.label: itertools.groupby reporting
prompts.text:
Explain why itertools.groupby requires sorted input for many use cases.
Build a toy report: given a list of dict rows with category, group and print subtotals.
Warn about iterator exhaustion pitfalls with groupby.
Offer an alternative using collections.defaultdict for confused beginners.
Keep the whole example under ~25 lines.

prompts.label: typing.Protocol structural duck typing
prompts.text:
Introduce typing.Protocol for "anything with a read()".
Show a function parse_payload(source: Readable) that accepts pathlib.Path.open() objects and io.StringIO.
Explain @runtime_checkable cautiously.
Contrast nominal inheritance vs structural typing.
Provide a minimal mypy-friendly example without advanced generics.

prompts.label: zipfile backups mini exercise
prompts.text:
Write a beginner script that bundles a handful of *.csv files into a dated zip archive using zipfile.
Include Path.glob, arcname basename handling, and a dry-run flag printed to the console.
Describe security warnings about unpacking untrusted zips at a high level.
Explain how I'd run this on Windows and macOS similarly.
Finish with ideas for scheduling later without implementing cron.
